item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I absolutely love this. It’s a surprising ray of hope in a hall of eldritch horrors and endless cycles of futility that even some of the sillier Skips present in their undertones. The Foundation has cracked the code of heaven, they can forcibly direct souls into it with some negotiation of other extra-dimensional entities and some good old-fashioned hard work. It’s an interesting look into the moral ambiguity that the Foundation employs in its mission, too. It’ll be interesting to imagine how they’d deploy this without lifting the veil/breaking the masquerade, without homogenizing the planet beyond the concept of protection, and without forcing it upon people who wish to abstain from an afterlife altogether.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: god this is such a great concept, but there are too many damn crosslinks. novote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I omitted the crosslink on purpose. I wanted to build intrigue / reward people who are deep into site lore with the satisfaction of puzzling it out themselves.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Where is GAW in this article anyway? I can't find the mention.

EDIT: It's Jude Kriyot (PoI-6870). I agree, the GAW tag should probably be removed, since Jude has her own tag and isn't acting in tandem with or on behalf of GAW in this article. Personally, i think a crosslink on "PoI-6870" would be appreciated, as I doubt I'm the only one who can't remember who everyone is by their PoI number.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This article is a year old but I feel it needs some criticism. The article is choppy at times and organizing the material might help its readability. For instance, the notes and clearance codes and quotes would work better if they were put together at the end. Also, several cross links and tags should be removed as most seem irrelevant to tangential at best, an example of this being the tagging of GAW, as they have little or no impact on this SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The crosslinks are a bit daunting, and this article can be a little hard to understand at certain points, but outside of that it is a good read. +1, but just barely.

Just one thing - This would make an awesome cannon or series of tales. There's so many possibilities with this concept of the R&D of a Foundation-created afterlife.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If there's no path to heaven open to the human race, the Foundation will damn well take care of that too, because those who die in the dark still deserve to see the light.

Inveniam viam aut faciam.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The main draw of this seems to be the "wow look how many crosslinks I used", which isn't holding up the core idea enough for me. There are a couple interesting uses, like using 1012 to wipe out 4009, but overall this ends up reading more like a tag search result page for 'death' or 'afterlife', with the bare minimum of text to show that you read them.

Combine that with the repeated trite buzzword- and cliche-filled Important Speeches in the notes, as well as the irritating levels of Foundationbabble, and this ends up being somehow less than the sum of its parts, for me.

(Also it's bizarre that you take the time to mention 3899's psychopomp qualities, when that tidbit is only mentioned in 3983, and there is not a single mention of that continuity's entire related afterlife struggle. Feels like a wasted opportunity.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like to think a group of the most qualified Senior Researchers spent years organizing this, and on one slip up, a Junior Researcher heppens upon a copy of this document and tells the researchers to just use SCP-3806

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So what I'm getting here is kinda like SCP-2000, except of reviving humanity, it brings them to an artificial paradise in the afterlife? It is even formatted in a similar manner to SCP-2000.

Also, I don't get a Thaumiel vibe here. Isn't the purpose of Thaumiel objects to contain other threats? I don't see that here really. I feel the Foundation wouldn't make this due to their goal to preserve and contain anomalies, not create or destroy afterlives. The latter sounds more like an action from the GOC. The premise sounds like a project of a GOI. Why would they plan to tell people about this too? This absolutely defeats the purpose of what the Foundation sets out to do. They are willing to come out and reveal an afterlife constructed by them? Even in SCP-2000, the folks get their minds wiped to preserve normalcy.

Anyway, now to the links. There is just too many cross links to view to understand the logs. It could easily turn a reader off because how overwhelming it is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh, when the saints, go marching in
Oh, when the saints go marching in
I want to be there in their number
When the saints go marching in

I'm reasonably sure that, at thirty six, I have the most crosslinks in any one article on the site.

Rejected names for Alpha-zero include:

  • Ọya-Iyansan
  • St. Peter's Psychopomps
  • Doormen of the Pearly Gates

Thanks to kinchtheknifeblade, OthellotheCat, DrBleep, Gabriel Jade, Captain Kirby, Tufto, 9Volt, Cyantreuse, Rimple, and RegularSlambo.
